sections.content: A Search and Rescue Transponder (SART) is a survival craft locating device that responds to radar interrogation signals. The traditional radar-SART operates on the 9 GHz X-band frequency. When interrogated by a ship's or aircraft's X-band radar, the SART transmits a series of response signals that appear as distinctive marks on the radar display, guiding rescuers directly to the survival craft.

The radar-SART display changes with distance. At long range, 12 equally-spaced dots appear on the radar screen, extending outward from the SART's position. As the searching vessel closes range, the dots stretch into arcs. Within approximately 1 nautical mile, the arcs merge into complete concentric circles centred on the SART. This progressive change provides the bridge team with a clear indication of both the SART's bearing and their closing distance.

rows: All AIS-equipped displays (radar overlay, ECDIS, standalone AIS)
Circle with X symbol on ECDIS
Depends on VHF propagation; typically similar range

sections.title: Radar-SART Detection Ranges
sections.content: A radar-SART can be detected at approximately 8 nautical miles by a ship's radar with a scanner mounted at 15 metres, and up to 30 nautical miles by an aircraft radar. The SART should be mounted at least 1 metre above sea level to maximise detection range.

The radar-SART battery must provide at least 96 hours in standby mode followed by 8 hours of continuous active operation when interrogated. Testing is performed using the built-in self-test function, which produces a single dot on the radar display (as opposed to the 12 dots of a live activation). Testing should be conducted briefly and only when no SAR operations are in progress to avoid confusion.

The AIS-SART is a modern alternative that operates on AIS VHF channels 87B and 88B instead of radar frequencies. Its key advantage is visibility: an AIS-SART appears on every AIS-equipped display on every vessel within range — not just on radar screens. This means that even vessels without someone actively watching the radar will receive an alert. The AIS-SART transmits 8 position reports per minute (4 on each channel) and carries the MMSI prefix 970. On ECDIS, it appears as a distinctive circle with an X symbol.

sections.title: Radar-SART Limitation
sections.content: A radar-SART only responds to X-band (9 GHz) radar. It will NOT respond to S-band (3 GHz) radar. Ensure searching vessels use their X-band radar when looking for radar-SARTs.

sections.prompt: At long range, a radar-SART appears on the radar display as:
sections.explanation: At long range, a radar-SART produces 12 equally-spaced dots on the radar screen. As the vessel approaches, the dots become arcs, and within about 1 NM they appear as complete concentric circles. A single dot indicates the self-test mode.

sections.prompt: The MMSI prefix for an AIS-SART is ___.
sections.explanation: AIS-SARTs are identified by the MMSI prefix 970. This unique prefix allows AIS receivers to recognise the transmission as a distress locating signal and display the appropriate SAR symbol (circle with X) on ECDIS.

sections.prompt: What is the key advantage of an AIS-SART over a traditional radar-SART?

options.text: It operates on S-band radar as well as X-band
It appears on all AIS-equipped displays, not just radar screens
It has a longer detection range than radar-SART

sections.explanation: The main advantage of an AIS-SART is that it appears on every AIS-equipped display (ECDIS, standalone AIS receivers, radar with AIS overlay) on every vessel within range, not just on the radar screen. This greatly increases the chance of detection.

points: Radar-SART operates on 9 GHz X-band and shows 12 dots (long range), arcs (medium), or circles (close range) on radar.
Radar-SART detection: ~8 NM by ship (15 m scanner), up to 30 NM by aircraft; mount at least 1 m above sea level.
Radar-SART battery: 96 hours standby + 8 hours active; self-test shows a single dot.
AIS-SART operates on AIS channels, appears on ALL AIS displays, MMSI prefix 970, symbol = circle with X.
AIS-SART transmits 8 messages per minute (4 per channel) with 96 hours of battery life.
